chunks.text: The proof is in adoption: dataclasses have become the default recommendation for new Python code. They're not just for "simple data containers"—they're better for virtually any class with fields. The boilerplate generation isn't sacrificing anything; it's eliminating redundancy while maintaining full class functionality.

meta.section: Why This Matters

chunks.text: **The question becomes**: If dataclasses are fundamentally better, why are they a decorator rather than the default?

meta.section: Why This Matters

chunks.text: The answer is Python's evolutionary constraint. With millions of lines of existing code using traditional classes, Python couldn't change the default behavior of the `class` keyword. Decorators were the pragmatic path to introduce better semantics without breaking existing code.

meta.section: Why This Matters

chunks.text: **Jac's advantage** is starting fresh. Without legacy constraints, `obj` makes dataclass-style semantics the natural way to define classes with fields:

meta.section: Why This Matters

chunks.text:
- **No decorator ceremony** - The better approach is built-in, not opt-in
- **Cleaner syntax** - Implicit `self`, `by postinit` instead of `field(init=False)`
- **Explicit intent** - `static has` distinguishes class variables from the instance variable default
- **Language-level features** - Access modifiers and instance variables as first-class concepts

meta.section: Why This Matters

chunks.text: If Python could redesign classes from scratch today, they'd likely look a lot more like Jac's `obj` than the current `class` syntax. Jac explores what becomes possible when you build the better design into the language from day one.

meta.section: Why This Matters

chunks.text:
## Comparison

| Aspect | Python `@dataclass` | Jac `obj` |
|--------|---------------------|-----------|
| Syntax | Decorator on `class` | `obj` keyword |
| Self parameter | Explicit in methods | Implicit in methods |
| Mutable defaults | `field(default_factory=list)` | `has items: list = []` works safely |
| Post-init | `field(init=False)` + `__post_init__` | `by postinit` + `postinit` |
| Static members | `class_var: ClassVar[int]` | `static has count: int` |
| Access control | Convention (`_private`) | Language feature (`:priv`) |

meta.section: Comparison
Comparison

chunks.text:
## Try It Yourself

Want to experiment with Jac's `obj` keyword?

meta.section: Try It Yourself

chunks.text:
```bash
pip install jaclang
```

meta.section: Try It Yourself

chunks.text: Create a file `objects.jac` with any of the examples above, then run:

meta.section: Try It Yourself

chunks.text:
```bash
jac run objects.jac
```

meta.section: Try It Yourself

chunks.text:
## Further Reading

- [PEP 557 - Data Classes](https://peps.python.org/pep-0557/)
- [Jac Language Documentation](https://www.jac-lang.org/)
- [Python dataclasses documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/dataclasses.html)

meta.section: Further Reading

chunks.text:
# You just have to learn 4 things to know Object-Spatial Programming

Most programming paradigms ask you to learn dozens of concepts, patterns, and best practices. Object-Spatial Programming (OSP) is different. At its core, there are really just **four fundamental concepts** you need to understand. Master these four things, and you'll have unlocked an entirely new way of thinking about computation—one that's particularly well-suited for modern applications dealing with complex relationships, graph structures, and distributed data.

meta.section: You just have to learn 4 things to know Object-Spatial Programming

chunks.text: This article will teach you the complete model of Object-Spatial Programming through these four conceptual buckets. Each builds naturally on the last, and by the end, you'll understand why OSP represents a genuine paradigm shift in how we structure programs.

meta.section: You just have to learn 4 things to know Object-Spatial Programming
You just have to learn 4 things to know Object-Spatial Programming

chunks.text:
## What is Object-Spatial Programming?

Before we dive into the four concepts, let's establish what makes Object-Spatial Programming fundamentally different from traditional Object-Oriented Programming (OOP).

meta.section: What is Object-Spatial Programming?

chunks.text:
**Traditional OOP:**
```
Data sits still → You bring computation to the data
(Call methods on objects)
```

meta.section: What is Object-Spatial Programming?

chunks.text:
**Object-Spatial Programming:**
```
Data is arranged in space → You send computation to travel through the data
(Walkers visit nodes in a graph)
```

meta.section: What is Object-Spatial Programming?

chunks.text: **The Restaurant Analogy**

meta.section: What is Object-Spatial Programming?

chunks.text: Think of it this way:

meta.section: What is Object-Spatial Programming?

chunks.text:
- **OOP**: You're at home, you call restaurants and have food delivered to you
- **OSP**: You send a robot out to visit different restaurants and collect food

meta.section: What is Object-Spatial Programming?

chunks.text: Both get you fed, but they work fundamentally differently! In OSP, computation is **mobile** and data is **spatial**.

meta.section: What is Object-Spatial Programming?
What is Object-Spatial Programming?

chunks.text:
## Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

The first thing you need to understand is how objects are structured in Object-Spatial Programming. Instead of thinking about isolated objects, you think about **objects arranged in space with explicit relationships**.

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: > **🔑 Core Insight:** Everything in OSP is built on classes. `node`, `edge`, and `walker` are **not** new primitive types—they're classes that inherit all OOP capabilities (methods, properties, inheritance, polymorphism) **and add** spatial/traversal semantics. This means OSP extends OOP rather than replacing it.

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: **The Two Pillars of the Spatial Object Model**

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text:
| Type | What It Is | Purpose | Traditional Analogy |
|------|------------|---------|-------------------|
| `node` | **Class with spatial semantics** | Data locations that can be connected in graphs | Houses on a map |
| `edge` | **Class representing relationships** | First-class connections between nodes | Roads between houses |

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: **Key point:** Nodes and edges are full-featured classes with methods, inheritance, and properties—PLUS spatial connection capabilities.

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: **Creating Nodes**

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: **Important: Nodes are classes!** They have all the semantics of regular classes (methods, inheritance, polymorphism) PLUS the ability to be connected in spatial graphs.

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: <div class="code-block">

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)
Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)
Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: At this point, nodes work exactly like regular classes. The spatial magic happens when we connect them...

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: **Connecting Nodes: The Spatial Operators**

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: This is where OSP diverges from traditional OOP. You can **directly connect** nodes using spatial operators:

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: <div class="code-block run-dot">

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)
Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)
Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: **Visual representation:**

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text:
```mermaid
graph TD
    Alice --> Bob
    Alice --> Charlie
    Bob --> Charlie
```

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: **Connection Operators Reference**

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text:
| Operator | Direction | Example | Result |
|----------|-----------|---------|--------|
| `++>` | Forward | `alice ++> bob;` | alice → bob |
| `<++` | Backward | `alice <++ bob;` | bob → alice |
| `<++>` | Both ways | `alice <++> bob;` | alice ↔ bob |

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: **Typed Edges: First-Class Relationships**

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: Here's what makes OSP truly revolutionary: **relationships are first-class objects** with their own types and properties!

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)

chunks.text: **Important: Edges are also classes!** Like nodes, edges have all the semantics of regular classes—they can have methods, properties, and behavior—PLUS they represent connections in the graph.

meta.section: Concept 1: The Spatial Object Model (Nodes & Edges)
